Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

args.code:
import json
import os
import re

citations_var = locals()['var_function-call-2106421940036629061']
papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

pattern = re.compile(r"(Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems)|(\bCHI\s+\d{4})|(\bCHI\s+'\d{2})", re.IGNORECASE)

print("Debugging A Trip to the Moon...")
for p in papers_list:
    if "Trip to the Moon" in p['filename']:
        text = p.get('text', '')
        print(f"Text length: {len(text)}")
        match = pattern.search(text)
        print(f"Regex match: {match}")
        header = text[:500].upper()
        print(f"Header contains UBICOMP: {'UBICOMP' in header}")
        
        # Check title match in citations
        title = p['filename'][:-4]
        found_in_citations = False
        for r in citations_data:
            if r['title'] == title:
                found_in_citations = True
                print(f"Citation count: {r['citation_count']}")
                break
        print(f"Found in citations: {found_in_citations}")
        break

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps("debug done"))

ianli@cmu.edu, {anind, forlizzi}@cs.cmu.edu

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

Fengjiao Peng

fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

import json
import os
import re

citations_var = locals()['var_function-call-2106421940036629061']
papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

pattern = re.compile(r"(Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems)|(\bCHI\s+\d{4})|(\bCHI\s+'\d{2})", re.IGNORECASE)

print("Debugging A Trip to the Moon...")
for p in papers_list:
    if "Trip to the Moon" in p['filename']:
        text = p.get('text', '')
        print(f"Text length: {len(text)}")
        match = pattern.search(text)
        print(f"Regex match: {match}")
        header = text[:500].upper()
        print(f"Header contains UBICOMP: {'UBICOMP' in header}")
        
        # Check title match in citations
        title = p['filename'][:-4]
        found_in_citations = False
        for r in citations_data:
            if r['title'] == title:
                found_in_citations = True
                print(f"Citation count: {r['citation_count']}")
                break
        print(f"Found in citations: {found_in_citations}")
        break

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps("debug done"))

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Fengjiao Peng
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating an Overactive Bladder App.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

Occupational Therapists in Stroke Rehabilitation 
Bernd Ploderer1,2, Justin Fong1,3, Anusha Withana1,4, Marlena Klaic1,5, Siddharth Nair1, 
Vincent Crocher1,3, Frank Vetere1, Suranga Nanayakkara1,4

2Queensland University of Technology, Brisbane, Australia

4Augmented Human Lab, Singapore University of Technology and Design, Singapore

{ploderer, vincent.crocher, f.vetere}@unimelb.edu.au, {justinf, siddharthn}@student.unimelb.edu.au, 
{anusha, suranga}@ahlab.org, marlena.klaic@mh.org.au

ABSTRACT 
This  paper  describes  the  design  of  “ArmSleeve”,  a  patient 
monitoring  system  to  support  occupational  therapists  in 
their  upper  limb  rehabilitation  work  with  stroke  patients.

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and Designing for Life After Personal Informatics Tool Use.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

import os
import re

papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

pattern = re.compile(r"(Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems)|(\bCHI\s+\d{4})|(\bCHI\s+'\d{2})", re.IGNORECASE)

debug_info = []

for p in papers_list:
    if "Trip to the Moon" in p['filename']:
        text = p.get('text', '')
        match = pattern.search(text)
        header = text[:500].upper()
        
        title = p['filename'][:-4]
        citation_count = 0
        for r in citations_data:
            if r['title'] == title:
                citation_count = r['citation_count']
                break
        
        debug_info.append({
            "title": title,
            "regex_match": str(match),
            "header_ubicomp": "UBICOMP" in header,
            "citation_count": citation_count
        })

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(debug_info))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

Fengjiao Peng

Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

import json
import os
import re

citations_var = locals()['var_function-call-2106421940036629061']
papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

pattern = re.compile(r"(Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems)|(\bCHI\s+\d{4})|(\bCHI\s+'\d{2})", re.IGNORECASE)

debug_info = []

for p in papers_list:
    if "Trip to the Moon" in p['filename']:
        text = p.get('text', '')
        match = pattern.search(text)
        header = text[:500].upper()
        
        title = p['filename'][:-4]
        citation_count = 0
        for r in citations_data:
            if r['title'] == title:
                citation_count = r['citation_count']
                break
        
        debug_info.append({
            "title": title,
            "regex_match": str(match),
            "header_ubicomp": "UBICOMP" in header,
            "citation_count": citation_count
        })

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(debug_info))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for

Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

import json
import os
import re

papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']
if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

pattern = re.compile(r"(Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems)|(\bCHI\s+\d{4})|(\bCHI\s+'\d{2})", re.IGNORECASE)

res = {}

for p in papers_list:
    if "Trip to the Moon" in p['filename']:
        text = p.get('text', '')
        # Find index of "CHI"
        idx = text.find("CHI")
        if idx != -1:
            snippet = text[idx:idx+20]
            res['snippet'] = snippet
            res['snippet_hex'] = snippet.encode('utf-8').hex()
            res['match'] = str(pattern.search(text))
        else:
            res['snippet'] = "CHI not found"
        break

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(res))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

Fengjiao Peng

Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating an Overactive Bladder App.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the

Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

var_function-call-17592913464419807258.title: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection

val_args.code:
import json
import os
import re

papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']
if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

pattern = re.compile(r"(Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems)|(\bCHI\s+\d{4})|(\bCHI\s+'\d{2})", re.IGNORECASE)

res = {}

for p in papers_list:
    if "Trip to the Moon" in p['filename']:
        text = p.get('text', '')
        # Find index of "CHI"
        idx = text.find("CHI")
        if idx != -1:
            snippet = text[idx:idx+20]
            res['snippet'] = snippet
            res['snippet_hex'] = snippet.encode('utf-8').hex()
            res['match'] = str(pattern.search(text))
        else:
            res['snippet'] = "CHI not found"
        break

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(res))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-

an Overactive Bladder App

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

args.code:
import json
import os
import re

citations_var = locals()['var_function-call-2106421940036629061']
papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

matched_papers = []

for p in papers_list:
    text = p.get('text', '')
    # Check if CHI is in text
    # Simple check first
    if "CHI" in text:
        # Get snippet
        idx = text.find("CHI")
        snippet = text[max(0, idx-20):min(len(text), idx+50)].replace('\n', ' ')
        
        # Heuristic: Check header for Venue
        header = text[:1000].upper()
        is_chi = False
        if "CHI" in header:
             # Could be "CHI" in title or "China" or "Machine" if case insensitive?
             # But I checked "CHI" (case sensitive).
             # "CHINA" contains "CHI".
             if "CHINA" in text[idx:idx+5]:
                 is_chi = False # Likely address
             elif "MACHINE" in text[idx-2:idx+5].upper():
                 is_chi = False
             else:
                 is_chi = True
        
        # Check copyright block (usually contains "Permission to make...")
        if "Permission to make" in text:
             perm_idx = text.find("Permission to make")
             perm_block = text[perm_idx:perm_idx+1000]
             if "CHI" in perm_block and "CHINA" not in perm_block:
                 is_chi = True
        
        # Check for UbiComp
        if "UBICOMP" in header:
            is_chi = False
            
        if is_chi:
            title = p['filename'][:-4] if p['filename'].endswith('.txt') else p['filename']
            citation_count = 0
            for r in citations_data:
                if r['title'] == title:
                    citation_count = int(r['citation_count'])
                    break
            
            matched_papers.append({
                "title": title,
                "snippet": snippet,
                "citations": citation_count
            })

total_citations = sum([m['citations'] for m in matched_papers])

print(f"Total Matches: {len(matched_papers)}")
print(f"Total Citations: {total_citations}")
print("Matches:")
print(json.dumps(matched_papers))

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(total_citations))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

val_args.code:
import json
import os
import re

citations_var = locals()['var_function-call-2106421940036629061']
papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

matched_papers = []

for p in papers_list:
    text = p.get('text', '')
    # Check if CHI is in text
    # Simple check first
    if "CHI" in text:
        # Get snippet
        idx = text.find("CHI")
        snippet = text[max(0, idx-20):min(len(text), idx+50)].replace('\n', ' ')
        
        # Heuristic: Check header for Venue
        header = text[:1000].upper()
        is_chi = False
        if "CHI" in header:
             # Could be "CHI" in title or "China" or "Machine" if case insensitive?
             # But I checked "CHI" (case sensitive).
             # "CHINA" contains "CHI".
             if "CHINA" in text[idx:idx+5]:
                 is_chi = False # Likely address
             elif "MACHINE" in text[idx-2:idx+5].upper():
                 is_chi = False
             else:
                 is_chi = True
        
        # Check copyright block (usually contains "Permission to make...")
        if "Permission to make" in text:
             perm_idx = text.find("Permission to make")
             perm_block = text[perm_idx:perm_idx+1000]
             if "CHI" in perm_block and "CHINA" not in perm_block:
                 is_chi = True
        
        # Check for UbiComp
        if "UBICOMP" in header:
            is_chi = False
            
        if is_chi:
            title = p['filename'][:-4] if p['filename'].endswith('.txt') else p['filename']
            citation_count = 0
            for r in citations_data:
                if r['title'] == title:
                    citation_count = int(r['citation_count'])
                    break
            
            matched_papers.append({
                "title": title,
                "snippet": snippet,
                "citations": citation_count
            })

total_citations = sum([m['citations'] for m in matched_papers])

print(f"Total Matches: {len(matched_papers)}")
print(f"Total Citations: {total_citations}")
print("Matches:")
print(json.dumps(matched_papers))

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(total_citations))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt

Fengjiao Peng

Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

args.code:
import json
import os
import re

citations_var = locals()['var_function-call-2106421940036629061']
papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

matched_papers = []

for p in papers_list:
    text = p.get('text', '')
    # Check if CHI is in text
    # Simple check first
    if "CHI" in text:
        # Get snippet
        idx = text.find("CHI")
        # Handle newlines in replace
        snippet = text[max(0, idx-20):min(len(text), idx+50)].replace('\\n', ' ')
        
        # Heuristic: Check header for Venue
        header = text[:1000].upper()
        is_chi = False
        
        # Check explicit venue mentions
        if "CHI 20" in text or "CHI '1" in text or "CHI '0" in text:
             # Likely CHI
             is_chi = True
        
        # Check exclusions
        if "UBICOMP" in header:
            is_chi = False
        if "DIS " in header:
            is_chi = False
            
        # Refine CHI check
        if is_chi:
            # Check if CHI is part of CHINA or MACHINE or ARCHI
            # We look at the "CHI" instance found
            # If "CHI 20" or "CHI '" is found, it is safe.
            pass
            
        if is_chi:
            title = p['filename'][:-4] if p['filename'].endswith('.txt') else p['filename']
            citation_count = 0
            for r in citations_data:
                if r['title'] == title:
                    citation_count = int(r['citation_count'])
                    break
            
            matched_papers.append({
                "title": title,
                "citations": citation_count
            })

total_citations = sum([m['citations'] for m in matched_papers])

# print(f"Total Matches: {len(matched_papers)}")
# print(f"Total Citations: {total_citations}")
# print("Matches:", json.dumps(matched_papers))

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(total_citations))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt

Fengjiao Peng

Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

val_args.code:
import json
import os
import re

citations_var = locals()['var_function-call-2106421940036629061']
papers_var = locals()['var_function-call-3764788576198629444']

if isinstance(citations_var, str) and os.path.exists(citations_var):
    with open(citations_var, 'r') as f:
        citations_data = json.load(f)
else:
    citations_data = citations_var

if isinstance(papers_var, str) and os.path.exists(papers_var):
    with open(papers_var, 'r') as f:
        papers_list = json.load(f)
else:
    papers_list = papers_var

matched_papers = []

for p in papers_list:
    text = p.get('text', '')
    # Check if CHI is in text
    # Simple check first
    if "CHI" in text:
        # Get snippet
        idx = text.find("CHI")
        # Handle newlines in replace
        snippet = text[max(0, idx-20):min(len(text), idx+50)].replace('\\n', ' ')
        
        # Heuristic: Check header for Venue
        header = text[:1000].upper()
        is_chi = False
        
        # Check explicit venue mentions
        if "CHI 20" in text or "CHI '1" in text or "CHI '0" in text:
             # Likely CHI
             is_chi = True
        
        # Check exclusions
        if "UBICOMP" in header:
            is_chi = False
        if "DIS " in header:
            is_chi = False
            
        # Refine CHI check
        if is_chi:
            # Check if CHI is part of CHINA or MACHINE or ARCHI
            # We look at the "CHI" instance found
            # If "CHI 20" or "CHI '" is found, it is safe.
            pass
            
        if is_chi:
            title = p['filename'][:-4] if p['filename'].endswith('.txt') else p['filename']
            citation_count = 0
            for r in citations_data:
                if r['title'] == title:
                    citation_count = int(r['citation_count'])
                    break
            
            matched_papers.append({
                "title": title,
                "citations": citation_count
            })

total_citations = sum([m['citations'] for m in matched_papers])

# print(f"Total Matches: {len(matched_papers)}")
# print(f"Total Citations: {total_citations}")
# print("Matches:", json.dumps(matched_papers))

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(total_citations))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Fengjiao Peng
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating an Overactive Bladder App.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

Occupational Therapists in Stroke Rehabilitation 
Bernd Ploderer1,2, Justin Fong1,3, Anusha Withana1,4, Marlena Klaic1,5, Siddharth Nair1, 
Vincent Crocher1,3, Frank Vetere1, Suranga Nanayakkara1,4

This  paper  describes  the  design  of  “ArmSleeve”,  a  patient

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and Designing for Life After Personal Informatics Tool Use.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Fengjiao Peng
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating an Overactive Bladder App.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

ABSTRACT 
This  paper  describes  the  design  of  “ArmSleeve”,  a  patient 
monitoring  system  to  support  occupational  therapists  in 
their  upper  limb  rehabilitation  work  with  stroke  patients. 
Occupational therapists can pro

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and Designing for Life After Personal Informatics Tool Use.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics.txt

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Fengjiao Peng
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating an Overactive Bladder App.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and Designing for Life After Personal Informatics Tool Use.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

args.code:
import json
import os

results_var = locals()['var_function-call-3493133027565418222']
if isinstance(results_var, str) and os.path.exists(results_var):
    with open(results_var, 'r') as f:
        results = json.load(f)
else:
    results = results_var

chi_papers = []
total = 0

for r in results:
    is_chi = False
    indicators = r['indicators']
    # Check if any indicator starts with "CHI" and seems to be the venue
    # We look for "CHI" indicator.
    # Be careful of "UBICOMP" indicator presence.
    
    has_chi = any("CHI" in ind for ind in indicators)
    has_ubicomp = any("UBICOMP" in ind for ind in indicators)
    has_dis = any("DIS" in ind for ind in indicators) # matches "DIS" string
    
    # "ArmSleeve" had ["DIS"]. So it's DIS.
    # "A Lived Informatics Model" had ["UBICOMP"].
    
    if has_chi and not has_ubicomp and not has_dis:
        is_chi = True
    elif has_chi and has_ubicomp:
        # Conflict. Check which one is venue.
        # "Beyond Abandonment" had "CHI" (CHI'16) but I thought it was UbiComp?
        # Let's check the indicator text.
        # If "CHI" indicator says "CHI'16" -> It's CHI.
        # If "UBICOMP" indicator says "UBICOMP '15" -> It's UbiComp.
        # A paper can't be both (usually).
        # "A Lived Informatics Model" had "UBICOMP '15". Did it have CHI?
        # My previous script output for it was: ["UBICOMP: UBICOMP '15..."]. No CHI.
        # So no conflict there.
        # If there is a conflict, we print it to decide.
        is_chi = True # Assume CHI if CHI is present and UbiComp is not (or if both present, handled below)
    
    # "ArmSleeve" has "DIS".
    # Check if "DIS" is "DIS 20.."?
    # My script just checked `if "DIS " in text`.
    # "DIS" might be "DIScussion".
    # But "ArmSleeve" had `indicators: ["DIS"]`.
    # Let's trust the "CHI" indicator more.
    
    if is_chi:
        chi_papers.append(r)
        total += r['count']

print(f"Total Citations: {total}")
print("CHI Papers:")
for p in chi_papers:
    print(f"{p['title']} ({p['count']}) - {p['indicators']}")

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(total))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt

Fengjiao Peng

Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo

A Wee Bit More Interaction: Designing and Evaluating  
an Overactive Bladder App

Ana-Maria Salai 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 as152@hw.ac.uk 

Lynne Baillie 
 Heriot-Watt University 
 Edinburgh, UK 
 l.baillie@hw.ac.uk

ABSTRACT 

Overactive  Bladder  (OAB)  is  a  widespread  condition, 
affecting  20%  of  the  population.  Even  though  it  is  a 
treatable  condition,  people  often  do  not  seek  treatment.  In 
this paper, we  describe how we co-designed and evaluated 
with  30  stakeholders  (9  medical  professionals  and  21  end-
users)  an  OAB  mobile  health  application  that  aims  to 
increase  adherence  to  self-managed  treatment.  Our  results 
support previous research that visualizing progress, setting 
goals, receiving reminders and feedback  increases  use.  We 
discovered  that  games  could  be  used  successfully  as  a 
distraction technique for urge suppression. Contrary to the 
current research direction, automatically calculated features

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and Designing for Life After Personal Informatics Tool Use.txt
var_function-call-11516224897388231770.text:
Beyond Abandonment to Next Steps: Understanding and 
Designing for Life after Personal Informatics Tool Use 

Daniel A. Epstein1, Monica Caraway2, Chuck Johnston2, 
An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {mcaraway, chuck2, anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Recent  research  examines  how  and  why  people  abandon 
self-tracking tools. We extend this work with new insights 
drawn from people reflecting on their experiences after they 
stop tracking, examining how designs continue to influence 
people  even  after  abandonment.  We  further  contrast  prior 
work  considering  abandonment  of  health  and  wellness 
tracking  tools  with  an  exploration  of  why  people  abandon 
financial  and  location  tracking  tools,  and  we  connect  our 
findings  to  models  of  personal  informatics.  Surveying  193 
people and interviewing

val_args.code:
import json
import os

results_var = locals()['var_function-call-3493133027565418222']
if isinstance(results_var, str) and os.path.exists(results_var):
    with open(results_var, 'r') as f:
        results = json.load(f)
else:
    results = results_var

chi_papers = []
total = 0

for r in results:
    is_chi = False
    indicators = r['indicators']
    # Check if any indicator starts with "CHI" and seems to be the venue
    # We look for "CHI" indicator.
    # Be careful of "UBICOMP" indicator presence.
    
    has_chi = any("CHI" in ind for ind in indicators)
    has_ubicomp = any("UBICOMP" in ind for ind in indicators)
    has_dis = any("DIS" in ind for ind in indicators) # matches "DIS" string
    
    # "ArmSleeve" had ["DIS"]. So it's DIS.
    # "A Lived Informatics Model" had ["UBICOMP"].
    
    if has_chi and not has_ubicomp and not has_dis:
        is_chi = True
    elif has_chi and has_ubicomp:
        # Conflict. Check which one is venue.
        # "Beyond Abandonment" had "CHI" (CHI'16) but I thought it was UbiComp?
        # Let's check the indicator text.
        # If "CHI" indicator says "CHI'16" -> It's CHI.
        # If "UBICOMP" indicator says "UBICOMP '15" -> It's UbiComp.
        # A paper can't be both (usually).
        # "A Lived Informatics Model" had "UBICOMP '15". Did it have CHI?
        # My previous script output for it was: ["UBICOMP: UBICOMP '15..."]. No CHI.
        # So no conflict there.
        # If there is a conflict, we print it to decide.
        is_chi = True # Assume CHI if CHI is present and UbiComp is not (or if both present, handled below)
    
    # "ArmSleeve" has "DIS".
    # Check if "DIS" is "DIS 20.."?
    # My script just checked `if "DIS " in text`.
    # "DIS" might be "DIScussion".
    # But "ArmSleeve" had `indicators: ["DIS"]`.
    # Let's trust the "CHI" indicator more.
    
    if is_chi:
        chi_papers.append(r)
        total += r['count']

print(f"Total Citations: {total}")
print("CHI Papers:")
for p in chi_papers:
    print(f"{p['title']} ({p['count']}) - {p['indicators']}")

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(total))

UBICOMP '15, SEPTEMBER 7–11, 2015, OSAKA, JAPAN

A Lived Informatics Model of Personal Informatics 

Daniel A. Epstein1, An Ping2, James Fogarty1, Sean A. Munson2 
1Computer Science & Engineering, 2Human Centered Design & Engineering 
DUB Group, University of Washington 
{depstein, jfogarty}@cs.washington.edu, {anping, smunson}@uw.edu 

ABSTRACT 
Current  models  of  how  people  use  personal  informatics 
systems are largely based in behavior change goals. They do 
not  adequately  characterize  the  integration  of  self-tracking 
into  everyday  life  by  people  with  varying  goals.  We  build 
upon  prior  work  by  embracing  the  perspective  of  lived 
informatics to propose a new model of personal informatics. 
We examine how lived informatics manifests in the habits of 
self-trackers across a variety of domains, first by surveying 
105, 99, and 83 past and present trackers of physical activity, 
finances, and location and then by interviewing 22 trackers 
regarding their

var_function-call-11516224897388231770.filename: A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for Self-reflection.txt

Fengjiao Peng

Cambridge, MA, USA
fpeng@mit.edu

A Trip to the Moon: Personalized Animated Movies for
Self-reﬂection
Veronica Crista LaBelle
MIT
Cambridge, MA, USA
vlabelle@mit.edu
Rosalind W. Picard
MIT Media Lab
Cambridge, MA, USA
picard@media.mit.edu

Emily Christen Yue
Harvard University
Cambridge, MA, USA
eyue@college.harvard.edu

ABSTRACT
Self-tracking physiological and psychological data poses the
challenge of presentation and interpretation. Insightful nar-
ratives for self-tracking data can motivate the user towards
constructive self-reﬂection. One powerful form of narrative
that engages audience across various culture and age groups
is animated movies. We collected a week of self-reported
mood and behavior data from each user and created in Unity a
personalized animation based on their data. We evaluated the
impact of their video in a randomized control trial with a non-
personalized animated video as control. We found that person-
alized videos tend to be mo
